coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-assisted preparation of water-borne reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline: From composite powder to layer-by-layer self-assembly film and performance enhancement
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Sumin
Zhang, Kai
Wang, Qiguan
Fan, Yaru
Shen, Jingwen
Li, Lu
Yang, Lei
Zhang, Wenzhi
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, aqueous solution of reduced graphene oxide has been prepared assisted by the graphene quantum dot, which is then mixed with aqueous polyaniline solution to form water-borne reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline composite powder. The resultant graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline shows a specific morphology of nanosized polyaniline firmly attached on reduced graphene oxide layer, due to the good dispersion of reduced graphene oxide and polyaniline in water. The monodispersion leads to the close contact between reduced graphene oxide sheets and polyaniline particles, significantly depressing the accumulation. It leads to the enhanced conductivity, supercapacitance and cycling stability for graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline, with capacitance as high as 648 F g−1. After 5000 charge-discharge cycles, the capacitance of as-prepared reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline composite shows just 3.7% decay while it reaches 68% for polyaniline. Furthermore, driven by the strong electrostatic forces between the electropositive polyaniline and the electronegative graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide as well as the π−π interactions, the obtained nanosized graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline films from the controllable layer-by-layer method show the morphology of interconnected-layer networks and exhibit good electrochemical activity on H2O2 in the range of 5.0 × 10−7–3.5 × 10−5 M. The detection limit is as low as 1.1 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot-graphene oxide
Water-borne polyaniline
Composite powder
Layer-by-layer self-assembly
Electrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for synthesis of reduced graphene oxide
Preparation of aqueous solution of GQD-rGO
Preparation of aqueous solution of conducting polyaniline
LbL self-assembly of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n on solid substrates
UV–visible analysis of GQD-rGO/PANI composite powder
UV–visible spectra of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films
XPS spectra of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films
SEM images of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films
Electrochemical catalytic activity of LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–visible spectra of PANI, GQD-rGO/PANI and GQD-rGO. (B) UV–visible spectrum variation of PANI solution (1.5 mg mL−1) by addition of different volumetric GQD-rGO solution (1.5 mg mL−1), a–k: 0, 5, 10, 20, 25, 30, 40, 70, 80, 180 and 200 μl. (C) UV–visible spectrum variation of PANI solution (1.5 mg mL−1) by addition of different volumetric aqueous acidic solution (pH = 3.0), a–f: 0, 10, 30, 75, 150 and 200 μl. (D) XRD features of GQD-rGO/PANI with different content of GQD-rGO, a: 0, b: 25 wt %, c: 33 wt %, d: 50 wt % and e: pure rGO.

SEM images of GQD-rGO (a), GQD-rGO/PANI with GQD-rGO of 25 wt % (b), 33 wt % (c), 50 wt % (e) and pure PANI (f), d shows the enlarged view of c. TEM images of GQD-rGO (g), GQD-rGO/PANI with GQD-rGO weight ratio of 33% (h) and 50% (i).

Electrical conductivity (A), galvanostatic charge-discharge (B) and the cycling behaviors (C) of GQD-rGO/PANI with different content of GQD-rGO, a: 0, b: 10 wt %, c: 25 wt %, d: 33 wt %, e: 50 wt % and f: pure GQD-rGO, and the schematic of interaction between GQD-rGO and PANI (D).

(A) UV–visible spectrum change of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n with the variation of self-assembly layer numbers, a–g: n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8 and 9. (B) Relationship between the self-assembly layer numbers and the UV–visible absorbance of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n at 317 nm and 630 nm. (C) The wide−range XPS spectrum and (D) the N 1s core−level XPS data of (GQD-rGO/PANI)8 film.

Cyclic voltammogram of bare ITO and (GQD-rGO/PANI)0.5/ITO (A) in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Cyclic voltammogram of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4) without (B) and with (C) addition of H2O2 (c = 10−4 mol dm−3), a–f: n = 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

(A) Cyclic voltammogram of (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4) containing H2O2 (c = 5 × 10−4 mol dm−3), a–i: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 and 150 mV s−1. (B) The linear relationship of peak current of H2O2 to the square root of scanning rate, a: oxidative peak current and b: reduction peak current. (C) Amperometric curve of (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO as the H2O2 concentration changes in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4), inset shows the amperometric behaviors in the H2O2 concentration range of 5 × 10−7–2 × 10−6 mol dm−3. (D) The linear relationship of peak current in the amperometric curves between the H2O2 concentration.

Amperometric response of (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO to the addition of H2O2 (10−4 mol dm−3), glucose (GLC, 10−3 mol dm−3), nifdipine (NIF, 10−4 mol dm−3), ascorbic acid (10−4 mol dm−3, AA) and dopamine (10−4 mol dm−3, DA) in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-assisted preparation of water-borne reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline: From composite powder to layer-by-layer self-assembly film and performance enhancement

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Functional Materials and Devices, School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Technological University, Xi'an, 710021, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Functional Materials and Devices
School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, aqueous solution of reduced graphene oxide has been prepared assisted by the graphene quantum dot, which is then mixed with aqueous polyaniline solution to form water-borne reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline composite powder. The resultant graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline shows a specific morphology of nanosized polyaniline firmly attached on reduced graphene oxide layer, due to the good dispersion of reduced graphene oxide and polyaniline in water. The monodispersion leads to the close contact between reduced graphene oxide sheets and polyaniline particles, significantly depressing the accumulation. It leads to the enhanced conductivity, supercapacitance and cycling stability for graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline, with capacitance as high as 648 F g−1. After 5000 charge-discharge cycles, the capacitance of as-prepared reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline composite shows just 3.7% decay while it reaches 68% for polyaniline. Furthermore, driven by the strong electrostatic forces between the electropositive polyaniline and the electronegative graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide as well as the π−π interactions, the obtained nanosized graphene quantum dot-reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline films from the controllable layer-by-layer method show the morphology of interconnected-layer networks and exhibit good electrochemical activity on H2O2 in the range of 5.0 × 10−7–3.5 × 10−5 M. The detection limit is as low as 1.1 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: The upcoming crises of the fossil resources exhaustion has motivated human seek for novel renewable energy [1,2]. As a promising kind of energy storage device, supercapacitors which includes electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and Faradaic pseudocapacitors have attracted more research attention [3,4]. Nanocarbon materials are currently used as the electrode materials for EDLCs, because of their large surface area, high conductivity and high stable structures [5]. Meanwhile, the Faradic pseudocapacitive materials such as metallic oxides and conducting polymers (CPs) showing higher capacitance than EDLC materials by means of their redox reactions in bulk, have also been widely investigated. Currently, hybridization of EDLC materials with pseudocapacitive electrode materials to fabricate mixed supercapacitors is becoming the trend in further improvement of the electrical conductivity, supercapacitance and structural stability of system [6,7].

As one of CPs showing a wide range of potential uses, polyaniline (PANI) has been composited with nanocarbons to pursue synergetic electrochemical or catalytic performances [8,9]. As to their synthesis, in−situ chemical or electrochemical polymerization of aniline/nanocarbons is generally employed [10–12]. Based on these methods, PANI composited with carbon nanotube (CNT) or reduced graphene oxide (rGO) showed the enhanced specified capacitance (∼500 F g−1), and the cycling stability of PANI was improved from 30% to 80%. Besides, it is reported that the rGO/PANI composites prepared by in−situ chemical oxidative or electrochemical polymerization method also displayed remarkable electrochemical catalytic activities to H2O2, with detection limit of 0.03 mM [13,14], which can be used as a non-enzymatic biosensor. Even so, compared with natural behaviors themselves, the performances of PANI/nanocarbons are still considered to be significantly confined, subjected to the aggregation of individual components. This is because in the process of in−situ chemical polymerization or electrochemical polymerization, the uncontrollable high-speed polymerization of aniline can induce the significant chain entangling before reaching the surface of nanocarbons [15]. On the other hand, the poor solubility of PANI and nanocarbons in aqueous systems also leads to their agglomeration. The serious clustering makes the nanocarbon/PANI interfacial interaction too weak, thus reducing the electrochemical performances of the bulk composites. Therefore, maintaining the monodispersed state of nanocarbon and PANI molecules during the composite construction becomes the key elements to reduce component agglomeration for further enhancing the electrochemical performance of resultant composites.

In this paper, we report a facile method to prepare water-borne rGO/PANI composites, by mixing the aqueous solution of rGO and PANI. Assisted by the graphene quantum dot (GQD), rGO was homogeneously dispersed in water, which was then mixed with PANI dispersed in an acidic aqueous solution to fabricate the rGO/PANI composite powder. Because the monodispersion of rGO and PANI improves the interfacial interaction, the resultant GQD-rGO/PANI composites showed a specific morphology of nanosized PANI attached on rGO layer. Furthermore, by using the strong electrostatic and the π−π interactions between the electropositive PANI and the electronegative GQD-rGO as the driving force, a layer-by-layer (LbL) self-assembly GQD-rGO/PANI film was constructed on the ITO or quartz slices. The homogeneous nanoscaled GQD-rGO/PANI powder and the uniform LbL film showed excellent electrochemical capacitance and catalytic behavior.

Natural flake graphite, glucose, nifdipine, ascorbic acid, dopamine and N, N-dimethyl acetamide were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., China. Carbon fibers (VGCF-H, diameter of about 150 nm and length of about 7 μm) were purchased from Showa Denko Industries, Japan. Hydrogen chloride (HCl, 37%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), nitric acid (HNO3, 68%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 28%), KMnO4 (AR), H3PO4 (AR), (NH4)2S2O8 (AR), acetone (AR) and ethanol (AR) were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Co., Ltd., and were used without further treatment. Aniline was used after distilled. ITO (10 × 10 cm, 5 Ω sq−1) and quartz glass were purchased from Changzhou Best Photoelectric Technology Co., China. The dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da was obtained from Beijing RuiDaHengHui Science & Technology Development Co, LTD, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for synthesis of reduced graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by a modified method based on Hummer's report [16]. Firstly 2 g of natural flake graphite was dispersed in the mixture composed of 45 mL concentrated H2SO4 and 5 mL of concentrated H3PO4 under sonication in an ice bath, during which 2 g KMnO4 was slowly added. Afterwards, the mixture temperature was increased to 40 °C. Then, 100 mL of high-purity water was added and stirred for 3 h. After acidic mixture was moved to an oil bath and heated at 85 °C for 1 h, 4 mL of H2O2 was added. The formed graphite oxide was immersed in 100 mL of the diluted HCl solution (1.0 M) for 24 h and filtered. After thoroughly washing with deionized water, the powdered graphite oxide in khaki was obtained by freeze drying. Upon heating at 600 °C for 2 h in argon, the graphite oxide was thermally reduced to graphene (rGO).

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized from the chemical oxidative method of carbon fiber exfoliation by mixed acids [17]. Briefly, 0.3 g of carbon fibers were added to the mixture containing 90 mL of concentrated H2SO4 and 30 mL of concentrated HNO3, and stirred for 24 h at 100 °C. Afterwards, the mixture solution was slowly poured into 800 mL of water and then was neutralized by aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (1.0 M). After thorough dialysis to remove small-sized ions in a dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da, the GQD powders were obtained by a distillation method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aqueous solution of GQD-rGO
ce:section.ce:para: Generally rGO alone cannot form stable dispersion in water because of the hydrophobic nature. Thus GQD was used to assist rGO dispersion in water by the strong π–π interactions between them. In this work, the aqueous solution of GQD-rGO was prepared by adding 15 mg of rGO and 15 mg of GQD in 15 mL of aqueous diluted HCl solution (pH = 3.0). After sonication, a significantly stable dispersion was obtained.

Polyaniline was synthesized by adding 15.0 g of ammonium persulfate to 60 mL of aqueous HCl solution (1.0 M) containing 6.0 g of aniline monomer [15]. The mixture was stirred at 0–5 °C for 24 h. Then the product was filtered and washed by diluted aqueous HCl solution, ethanol and deionized water. After immersed in aqueous ammonia solution (1.0 M) for 10 h, the obtained emeradine was dried under vacuum at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aqueous solution of conducting polyaniline
ce:section.ce:para: By adding 200 mg of emeradine polyaniline in 10 mL of N, N-dimethyl acetamide, a dark blue homogeneous solution was obtained after stirred for 2 h [18]. Subsequently, the aqueous conducting polyaniline solution can be gained by adding above PANI solution in 50 mL of diluted aqueous HCl solution (pH = 3.0), which was stored for LbL self-assembly.

To 20 mL of as-prepared aqueous PANI solution, 100 mL of water (pH = 3.0) was added under stirring, then 3 mL GQD-rGO solution was added. After stirred for 4 h, the mixed system was filtered and the GQD-rGO/PANI composite powder was obtained after drying in vacuum, in which the weight content of GQD-rGO is 10%. Similarly, the powders with different weight content of GQD-rGO such as 25%, 33% and 50% can be prepared.

By using electrostatic and π−π interactions as the driving force, GQD-rGO and PANI were alternatively deposited on the solid substrates such as ITO and quartz slices to fabricate the (GQD-rGO/PANI)n LbL self-assembly film. The ITO or the quartz plates were firstly cut into 0.5 × 2 cm slices and thoroughly cleaned by ultrasonication in ethanol, acetone and DI water, and dried by nitrogen blow. In order to produce hydroxyl groups uniformly distributed on the solid surface, the quartz or the ITO plates were hydrolyzed in an oxidative solution containing 10 mL of H2O2, 10 mL of NH4OH and 50 mL of H2O for 30 min at 80 °C, then in a mixture of H2O/HCl/H2O2 (6:1:1, v/v/v) for 30 min under ultrasonication. Afterwards, they were cleaned thoroughly with high-purity water and dried by nitrogen blow [19]. The hydroxyl functionalized plate was then moved to an oven at 100 °C, and dried for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LbL self-assembly of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n on solid substrates
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate (GQD-rGO/PANI)n LbL self-assembly film, a slice of hydroxyl functionalized ITO or quartz glass was immersed in the aqueous PANI solution for 2 h, then take out and rinsed thoroughly by water [20,21]. Afterwards, the solid substrate was dried by nitrogen blow at room temperature. To induce the GQD-rGO deposit on the glass surface, the glass was additionally immersed in the aqueous GQD-rGO solution for 2 h, then one layer of GQD-rGO/PANI was formed on the solid substrate, which can be denoted as (GQD-rGO/PANI)1. According to the similar procedure, LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n films with favored layers were fabricated on the solid substrates.

For capacitance measurement, the electroactive GQD-rGO/PANI powders were firstly mixed with 15 wt % carbon black, and 5 wt % polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF). The formed dispersion was coated on a plate of stainless steel mesh and dried at 80 °C for 2 h, followed by a pressed process to obtain the supercapacitive electrode [7]. Two electrodes sandwiched by a polyethylene-polypropylene film were placed in a CR2016 coin-type battery case, which was sealed after addition of H2SO4 solution (1.0 M).

The interactions and the LbL process of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n were detected by using UV–visible spectra measurement on a Shimadzu UV−2550 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT−IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 750 FT−IR spectrometer using the pressed KBr pellets in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. X−ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the GQD-rGO/PANI powders was conducted on Shimadzu XRD−6000 diffractometer. The surface elements of GQD-rGO/PANI composites were taken on a Kratos AXIS X−ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Electrical conductivity was determined using a four-point probe method at room temperature. Five measurements were performed and averaged on each sample to give the final data. Morphologies of the GQD-rGO/PANI were examined using a Hitachi S−4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a JEOL JSM 2010 transmission electron microscope (TEM).

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) characterization of LbL self-assembly (GQD-rGO/PANI)n films was carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660) in a conventional three−electrode system, with an Ag/AgCl/3M KCl electrode as the reference electrode and a platinum foil as the counter electrode. The (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO plate was used as working electrode, with the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) solution (pH = 7.4) as the electrolyte. Capacitance measurement of the GQD-rGO/PANI powders was performed on a Neware battery test machine (CT3008W, Shenzhen, China). The measured current density (A g−1) and the calculated capacitance (F g−1) are all referred to the active materials of GQD-rGO/PANI deposited on the electrodes. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–100 kHz at the open-circuit potential difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible analysis of GQD-rGO/PANI composite powder
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the interfacial interactions between GQD-rGO and PANI, the UV–visible spectra of diluted aqueous solution of GQD-rGO/PANI were detected. As illustrated from Fig. 1 A, GQD-rGO showed no features in the range 300–800 nm due to the reduction of oxygen functionalized groups on rGO. PANI displayed the characteristic absorption peak of π−π* at 326 nm and the polaron peak at 616 nm [22]. However, in the spectrum of GQD-rGO/PANI, the absorption of PANI at 326 and 616 nm red shifted to 337 nm and 634 nm. This indicates the strong π−π interactions happened at the interfaces between PANI and rGO, leading to the enhancement of the electron delocalization ability for the conjugate rings on PANI backbone.

To find the effect of the mass ratio of GQD-rGO to PANI on the interfacial interaction strength, dropwise addition of varied volume of GQD-rGO solution (1.5 mg mL−1) to PANI (5 mL, 1.5 mg mL−1) was carried out. As the addition of GQD-rGO solution, it can be shown from Fig. 1B that the strength of characteristic absorption peak of PANI located at 326 and 616 nm become weaker and weaker, at the same time, accompanied by the gradual red shift of peak position (see the arrow in Fig. 1B). This phenomenon further demonstrates that there existed strong π−π interactions between PANI and rGO, which enhanced the electron delocalization of PANI backbone [23]. In addition, for comparison, the experiment of adding acidic solution (pH = 3) to the PANI solution was proceeded, for the sake of detecting the effect of the dispersed media on the UV–visible absorption. As found from Fig. 1C, the absorption strength almost kept unchanged during stepwise addition of acidic water in PANI solution.

Fig. 1D shows the XRD curves of GQD-rGO/PANI with different mass ratio of GQD-rGO. For pure PANI, two wide diffraction peaks can be found at 2θ angle 19.6° and 23.5°, attributed to diffraction from the (100), (110) and (200) plane [24]. The weak strength at the wide peaks shows that the crystallinity is relatively low. In addition, the broad diffraction peaks seen around 20° and 26.7° in the XRD pattern of GQD-rGO implies the removal of oxygen-containing groups on the basal planes of rGO by the reduction of GO, which attribute to the (002) crystalline plane of graphite [25]. For the GQD-rGO/PANI composites, the XRD patterns are similar to the combination of GQD-rGO and PANI diffraction patterns, indicating that GQD-rGO and PANI is well composited during solution mixing process, without crystalline structural changes.

Fig. 2 a−f and 2g−I illustrate the SEM and TEM images of GQD-rGO, PANI and GQD-rGO/PANI, respectively. As shown from Fig. 2a, GQD-rGO shows a layer-like structure, composed of randomly clustered sheets. From the transparent edges of rGO layer, it can be discerned the thickness of every sheet is very thin (Fig. 2g, TEM image). In addition, abundant winkles on the smooth surface illustrated its flexibility nature of rGO (Fig. 2a). In the sample of GQD-rGO/PANI with 25 wt % GQD-rGO (Fig. 2b), large-sized PANI particles (about 670 nm) were found. This is because the electrostatic and π−π interactions between rGO and PANI were relatively weak in the presence of low GQD-rGO content, that it cannot effectively retard the aggregation of PANI and rGO themselves. As the GQD-rGO content increases to 33 wt %, the PANI particles were homogeneously attached on the rGO surface (see the arrow in Fig. 2c). In addition, the particle size of PANI was seriously reduced under the electrostatic and π−π interactions between GQD-rGO and PANI. The maximum size was decreased to around 350 nm (see the bars in Fig. 2d and h). Additionally, with the further increase of interaction strength between GQD-rGO and PANI as in the case of GQD-rGO/PANI with 50 wt % GQD-rGO, the PANI particles become much smaller and more uniformly distributed on the rGO surface, observed from the SEM (Fig. 2e) and TEM (Fig. 2i) images, compared with pure PANI (Fig. 2f). From the FT−IR spectra shown in Fig. S1, the characteristic peaks assigned to PANI for GQD-rGO/PANI samples illustrated remarkable frequency shift towards lower value, compared with pure PANI. This is because the strong π−π and electrostatic interactions within the composites induced deformation of the involved bonds. Also, different shift values are found for the GQD-rGO/PANI with different GQD-rGO content, due to the strength variance of π−π and electrostatic interactions. Therefore, it can be concluded that the π−π and electrostatic interactions within GQD-rGO/PANI composites are the main driving forces for the morphological evolution observed from SEM and TEM images.

The electrical conductivity of GQD-rGO/PANI composites was detected at room temperature by four−probe method, as shown by the histogram in Fig. 3 A. The pure PANI and pure GQD-rGO showed the electrical conductivity of 0.7143 and 0.5286 S cm−1, respectively. However, by mixing GQD-rGO with PANI, the formed GQD-rGO/PANI composites showed varied conductivity as the mass content of GQD-rGO varied. In the composite with 10 wt % GQD-rGO, the electrical conductivity of GQD-rGO/PANI samples is 0.01205 S cm−1. It was increased to 0.05155 and 0.2273 S cm−1 when the weight content of GQD-rGO increased to 25% and 33% respectively. Interestingly, it increased to 0.8333 S cm−1 when the mass content of GQD-rGO increased to 50%. It is higher than that of both pure PANI and GQD-rGO. This is because with the weight content increase of GQD-rGO, the electrostatic and the π−π interactions between PANI and rGO were gradually enhanced, and the morphology change of PANI from particle cluster to uniform distribution results in a well-connected conductive network, as shown from SEM images in Fig. 2.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge behavior of GQD-rGO/PANI samples was investigated at 1.0 A g−1 current density. The obtained galvanostatic charge-discharge curves were illustrated in Fig. 3B. It can be seen that the energy-storage capacity of GQD-rGO mainly come from the electrical double-layer capacitance, discerned from the symmetric triangular shape of the charge-discharge curve. While for the PANI and GQD-rGO/PANI composites, the energy-storage performances are found to be from the pseudocapacitance due to the redox reactions in bulk PANI, based on the asymmetric triangular shape of the charge-discharge curve. Due to the pseudocapacitance, PANI showed a higher capacitance (194 F g−1) than GQD-rGO (100 F g−1). Furthermore, compared with pure PANI, the GQD-rGO/PANI composites exhibited larger specific capacitance. For GQD-rGO/PANI with 10 wt %, 25 wt % and 33 wt % GQD-rGO, the capacitance is 394, 472 and 608 F g−1 calculated by the reported method [26]. This is because the introduction of GQD-rGO in PANI matrix increased the conductive pathways formed via the electrostatic and the π−π interactions between GQD-rGO and PANI as shown in Fig. 3D, which facilitates electron motion.

Because of the high-velocity electron motion supported by the well-connected conductive pathways, the composites containing 50 wt % GQD-rGO showed the largest capacitance value of 648 F g−1. It should be noted that it is much higher than that of graphene/PANI prepared by solution mixing (∼579.8 F g−1) [27,28], and that of graphene/PANI composites from chemical oxidative polymerization (∼555 F g−1) [29–31], interfacial polymerization (79.5 F g−1) and pickering emulsion polymerization (487.2 F g−1) [32]. In addition, based on the enhanced interfacial contact induced by the morphological changes, the GQD-rGO/PANI samples containing 50 wt % GQD-rGO showed the lowest charge transfer resistance (Rct), detected by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (Fig. S2).

Meanwhile, the GQD-rGO/PANI samples also showed improved cycling stability, compared with pure PANI. The GQD-rGO/PANI composite electrodes were repeatedly charged and discharged for 5000 cycles at 1.0 A g−1 (Fig. 3C). From Fig. 3C, it can be seen after 5000 cycles, GQD-rGO/PANI with 50 wt % GQD-rGO displayed the highest capacitance retention of 96.3% among the measured composite samples. While for pure PANI, it is found to be only 32%. In addition, even at a much higher current density of 10.0 A g−1, the GQD-rGO/PANI composite with 50 wt % GQD-rGO can yet show a low capacitance decay of 14% after 5000 cycles (Fig. S3). It is also lower than the reported rGO/PANI and GO/PANI composites (16–20%) from chemical oxidative polymerization method [32–34]. This indicates that, the introduction of strong electrostatic and π−π interactions between GQD-rGO and PANI significantly enhanced their interfacial contact and effectively reduced the mechanical strain, swelling, cracks of PANI during cyclic charge-discharge process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible spectra of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films
ce:section.ce:para: By using the electrostatic adsorption of the positive charged PANI and the negative charged GQD-rGO as well as the π−π interactions, LbL self-assembly GQD-rGO/PANI films were prepared on the hydroxyl terminated quartz and ITO slices via the alternatively dipping method. The UV–visible spectra of (GQD-rGO/PANI)n (n is the number of the self-assembly layers) were shown in Fig. 4 A. From Fig. 4A, it can be found that the characteristic absorbance of PANI located at ∼311 and ∼630 nm was gradually enhanced with the increase of self-assembly layer numbers, accompanied by the red-shift of the peak position (see the arrows in Fig. 4A), because of the electrostatic and π−π interactions between rGO and PANI. Fig. 4B shows the linear relationship between the peak absorbance located at ∼311 and ∼630 nm and the self-assembly layer number. It indicates that the growth of GQD-rGO/PANI layers is uniform and continuous driven by the electrostatic and π−π interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS spectra of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the interactions between GQD-rGO and PANI, XPS spectra of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films on quartz slices were measured. From the wide−range XPS spectra of GQD-rGO/PANI films in Fig. 4C, the N 1s signal indicated the presence of PANI on quartz plates. In addition, the N1s core−level spectrum of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI film can be deconvoluted into four major peaks centered at 398.6 eV (−NH = ), 399.3 eV (−NH−), 400.1 eV (−NH+−) and 402.0 eV (−NH+ = ) (Fig. 4D) respectively [35,36]. The features of nitrogen cation and iminium ions indicate there exist strong electrostatic interactions between GQD-rGO and PANI as shown in Fig. 3D, besides the π−π interaction observed from UV–visible spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM images of LbL GQD-rGO/PANI films
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n films was examined by SEM measurement, as shown in Fig. 5 . From the SEM image of the pure PANI layer [denoted as (GQD-rGO/PANI)0.5, Fig. 5a], the PANI particles can be found to be loosely distributed on the quartz surfaces, showing the small loading amount of PANI. By additional adsorption of GQD-rGO layer [denoted as (GQD-rGO/PANI)1, Fig. 5b], the PANI particles were covered by rGO nanoribbons. Because the loaded PANI and GQD-rGO amount is both low, the coverage degree of quartz surface is low. As the self-assembly layers increase, such as the (GQD-rGO/PANI)5, (Fig. 5c), the LbL films become much denser. As the LbL layer number reaches 10 (Fig. 5d), a uniformly inter-connected network composed of PANI nanoparticles attached on GQD-rGO sheets was found. However, as the further increase of LbL layer numbers to 15 and 25, large-scale clustering of rGO occurred, as shown by the arrows in Fig. 5e and the ellipse in Fig. 5f. Therefore, the uniformly inter-connected layer structure of particle-attached sheets in (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO may support good electrochemical activities, indicated by the abundant nanopores formed (see the arrows in Fig. 5d), which benefit the ion adsorption and motion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical catalytic activity of LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 displays the typical CV curves observed in N2−saturated PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) on the LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO (n = 0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20) at the scan rate of 0.05 V s−1. For bare ITO and (GQD-rGO/PANI)0.5/ITO (Fig. 6A), there present no obvious peaks, because of the scarcity of electroactive materials on the ITO plates. However, in the case of (GQD-rGO/PANI)1/ITO (curve a, Fig. 6B), a pair of redox peak at 0.05/-0.2 vs (Ag/AgCl)/V was found, corresponding to the oxidation and reduction of polyaniline [13]. Also, it was found with the increase of self-assembly layers, the responded current on the LbL films was enhanced, owing to the improved loading amount of electroactive materials on ITO plates. However, when the self-assembly layer number reaches 25, the redox current was significantly reduced (curve f, Fig. 6B). This is probably because the film is too dense in this case (Fig. 5f), which blocked the electron transfer from electrolyte across the film to the surface of ITO. By addition of hydrogen peroxide (10−4 M) in the PBS solution, the CV peak currents (Fig. 6C) corresponding to the LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO were increased compared with the value in Fig. 6B. For n = 1, 5, 10, 15 and 20, the peak current increase on the (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO is 2.2%, 23.75%, 89.6%, 84.7% and 25.3%, respectively. This showed there was remarkable catalysis towards hydrogen peroxide on the LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)n/ITO plates, among which the LbL (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO possesses the highest response towards hydrogen peroxide. Meanwhile, the redox current of (GQD-rGO/PANI)25/ITO (curve f, Fig. 6C) was also significantly decreased, similar to the case without addition of H2O2 (curve f, Fig. 6B), because the dense surface limited the electron transfer.

In addition, it can be found that with increasing scanning rate, oxidation potential of hydrogen peroxide (5 × 10−4 mol dm−3) at the surface of the (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO was positively shifted (see arrow in Fig. 7 A), along with peak current enlargement (Fig. 7B). Furthermore, both the oxidative (curve a in Fig. 7B) and the reductive (curve b in Fig. 7B) peak current of hydrogen peroxide illustrated a proportional relation to the square root of scan rate ranged from 10 to 150 mV s−1, which can be concluded that the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide on the (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO electrode should be a diffusion controlled process [37].

We further examined the electrocatalytic response of (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO electrode to the varied H2O2 concentration. Fig. 7C shows the systematic increase in the reduction peak current by increasing the H2O2 concentration ranged from 5 × 10−7 to 1.95 × 10−4 mol dm−3. The peak current versus concentration plot presents a good linearity for H2O2 reduction in the range from 5.0 × 10−7 to 3.5 × 10−5 mol dm−3 (Fig. 7D), which can be expressed by the following equation, with correlation coefficients of 0.9986:
Ip(A) = − 0.016 C − 0.26 ×
10
− 6
;
r
2
= 0.9986
where C represents the H2O2 concentration (μM). From the standard deviation (σ) and the slope of regression line (b), calculated by using the formula 3σ/b [38,39], the quantification and detection limit for the (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO sensor was found to be 1.1 × 10−6 M. Among the reported non-enzyme sensors for hydrogen peroxide detection [15,40–42], the (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO sensor in this work showed superior detection limit for H2O2, due to the high catalytic activity from the specific self-assembly layer structures.

The selectivity of the (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO for the H2O2 detection was also evaluated. Fig. 8 shows the current responses of the (GQD-rGO/PANI)10/ITO to 10−4 M H2O2, 10−3 M glucose (GLC), 10−4 M nifdipine (NIF), 10−4 M ascorbic acid (AA) and 10−4 M dopamine (DA). There exists obvious current response in the case of adding 10−4 M H2O2. On the contrary, no obvious current response is found with adding 10−3 M GLC, 10−4 M NIF, 10−4 M AA and 10−4 M DA. These results suggest that under above testing conditions, the interfering effect of those biomaterials on the electro-catalysis current response of H2O2 can be nearly negligible. The good ability of anti-interference ensures the availability of this LbL GQD-rGO/PANI sensor.

In summary, by the assistance of the graphene quantum dot (GQD), aqueous solution of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was prepared. By mixing GQD-rGO with aqueous polyaniline (PANI) solution, the water-borne rGO/PANI composite powder was obtained, which showed a specific morphology of nanosized PANI attached on rGO layer, due to the strong electrostatic and π−π interactions between rGO and PANI. The nanosized PANI makes the GQD-rGO/PANI system show increased bulk conductivity and supercapacitance, with capacitance as high as 648 F g−1. Also, the capacitive cycling performance of rGO/PANI was greatly enhanced. After 5000 charge-discharge cycles, the capacitance of reduced graphene oxide/polyaniline shows just 3.7% decay while it reaches 68% for polyaniline. Furthermore, linked by the strong electrostatic forces between the electropositive PANI and the electronegative GQD-rGO as well as the π−π interactions, the controllable nanosized layer-by-layer (LbL) self-assembly GQD-rGO/PANI films, prepared by repetitive soaking ITO or quartz slices into aqueous GQD-rGO and aqueous PANI solution, showed significant electrochemical activity on H2O2 with the detection limit as low as 1.1 μM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.M. acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21772152, 21103133); the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, State Education Ministry.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.10.135.
